title: Fitting a draught-proof threshold strip to an external door
subtitle: Replace or fit a brush or rubber-fin draught seal at the bottom of an external door to stop cold air and reduce heat loss
excerpt: How to fit a draught-proof threshold strip to an external door. Covers choosing between brush-seal and rubber-fin types, trimming the door bottom if needed for clearance, and screwing the strip in place for an effective seal.
sourceNotes: UK draught-proofing guidance from the Energy Saving Trust and Collins Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). UK building trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A draught seal fitted to the bottom of an external door or door frame to block the gap between the door bottom and the threshold. Two common types: a brush seal, in which a nylon-bristle strip is recessed into or surface-mounted on the door bottom edge and sweeps the floor as the door moves; and a rubber-fin or silicone-bubble seal, in which a flexible fin compresses against the threshold as the door closes. Brush seals work on uneven floors; rubber-fin seals give a tighter seal on flat thresholds.

content.text: stops cold air entering at the bottom of an external door. The approach is choosing the right seal type for the floor surface, cutting to the door width, and screwing the strip to the door bottom or frame. Most external doors can be sealed in under an hour without removing them.
Assess the gap and choose the seal type

Close the door and slide a piece of paper under it at several points. Where the paper slides freely, there is no effective seal. A gap under 5 mm on an uneven or textured floor is best handled by a brush seal (the bristles conform to surface irregularities. A flat, consistent threshold with a gap of 3 to 8 mm works well with a rubber-fin or silicone-bubble type, which compresses more firmly to give a better seal.

Measure from the door bottom to the floor at the closed position. A surface-mounted strip adds 8 to 12 mm to the door bottom. If the door currently has 5 mm clearance, the strip will drag badly or jam on a mat. If clearance is less than the strip height, trim the door bottom with a plane or a saw before fitting. Plane or saw a single timber door cold; use a circular saw on composite or foam-core doors.

Cut the strip to the door width) most aluminium-backed brush seals cut with a hacksaw or fine-tooth saw. Mark the cutting line squarely with the combination square. Position the strip on the inside face of the door bottom, centred on the door width, so the brush or fin just touches the threshold when the door is closed. Mark the screw positions and drill pilot holes before driving the screws: the backing is aluminium and the holes need pre-drilling to avoid splitting the strip.

Close the door and slide the paper test piece under again. The paper should meet firm resistance across the full width. If the brush rides over the threshold without pressing, lower the strip by 2 to 3 mm by elongating the screw slots slightly with the utility knife and re-fixing.
